notes.note: Stabiae, the coastal town south of Pompeii on the Bay of Naples; M. Marius's villa had a window cut in the bedroom-wall to its view.
The lavish dedication-games of Pompey's stone theatre, the first permanent theatre at Rome (in the Campus Martius), held in late September 55 BC.
Sp. Maecius Tarpa, the Roman dramatic critic; under whose curation the stage-plays at Pompey's games were chosen. His taste was the offence.
``If I knowingly deceive'' — the Roman oath formula. Aesopus, the great tragic actor and Cicero's friend, was elderly by 55 BC; his voice failing on this very line was a marked moment.
Accius's lost tragedy, revived at the games with a triumphal procession of six hundred mules and three thousand mixing-bowls — the kind of stage-overload Cicero deplored.
Pompey's games closed with a slaughter of elephants in the Circus, on the last (fifth) day of the venatio. The crowd, unexpectedly, took pity on them, and the show backfired (cf. Pliny NH 8.21).
C. Caninius Gallus, tribune of the plebs 56 BC, defended by Cicero on a maiestas charge in 55 BC. Cicero ``almost burst himself'' (\textit{dirupi me paene}) defending him.
``To live as a human being'' — the famous Ciceronian phrase, the goal of \textit{otium cum dignitate}. Cicero will, when he is at last free, learn from Marius this art of leisure.
